title: Removing and replacing ceramic wall tiles
subtitle: Chisel out the damaged tile, prepare the surface, set the new one
excerpt: How to remove a cracked or stained ceramic wall tile and replace it with a matching one: protecting surrounding tiles, chiselling out the old adhesive, preparing the wall surface, and bedding the replacement tile level with its neighbours.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. British Standards BS 5385 (wall and floor tiling). City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling syllabus.
aliases: how to replace a cracked tile

glossaryTerms.definition: A cement-based or ready-mixed paste that bonds ceramic, porcelain, and natural stone tiles to walls and floors. Wall adhesive is non-slump; floor adhesive is stiffer.

content.text: Replacing a single cracked or stained ceramic tile is a worthwhile repair that avoids retiling an entire surface. The job works when matching tiles are available and the wall behind is sound. If the wall behind is crumbling or soft when tapped, the problem is water damage behind the tiles; replacing one tile will not solve it.
Wear safety goggles throughout. A ceramic tile struck with a chisel can shatter and throw fragments at eye level.
What you'll need
Matching replacement tile.
Bring a fragment of the old tile to a tile merchant to match the size and surface. Old tiles may be thicker or thinner than modern stock; check the depth before buying.
Cold chisel and club hammer.
For breaking out the old tile and removing the adhesive bed.
Tile adhesive, wall grade.
A 500 g ready-mixed tub is enough for a single tile.
Grout to match.
Wall grout in the closest colour to the existing. Buy a small sachet if the existing grout colour is standard.
Safety goggles, 50 mm scraper, filling knife.
Grout raker or craft knife.
For removing the old grout from the joints before chiselling.
Removing the old tile
Step 1, remove the grout from all four joints.

Rake out the grout around the damaged tile with a grout raker or the corner of a craft knife. Clear the full depth of the joint on all four sides. If the grout is left in place, the cold chisel will transfer impact to the surrounding tiles and may crack them.
Step 2, score the tile face.

Score an X across the face of the tile with a craft knife or tile scribe to provide a starting point for the chisel. This also reduces the chance of the tile shattering outward rather than breaking inward.
Step 3, break and remove the tile.

Hold the cold chisel at the centre of the tile and strike firmly with the hammer. The tile should break inward. Work from the broken centre outward toward the edges, lifting fragments away with the scraper. Avoid striking hard at the corners, where the chisel is closest to the adjacent tiles.
Step 4, clean the wall behind.
Chip away the old
tile adhesive

from the wall with the scraper and chisel. The wall behind should be flat and sound, with no loose plaster. Fill any deep voids with patching plaster and allow to dry before proceeding. The new tile must sit at the same depth as the surrounding tiles.
Setting the replacement tile
Step 5, apply adhesive to the back of the tile.

Spread a 5 mm layer of wall-grade adhesive onto the back of the replacement tile with the filling knife, pressing it into the tile surface. Applying to the tile back (back buttering) rather than the wall gives better control of the tile depth for a single replacement.
Step 6, set the tile.

Press the tile firmly into the opening, centring it in the gap. The tile face should be level with the surrounding tiles; press harder or pull back and add more adhesive if it is proud or recessed. Insert tile spacers on all four sides to maintain the grout joint width. Allow the adhesive to cure fully (minimum 24 hours) before grouting.
Step 7, grout the joints.

Mix the grout to a smooth, stiff paste. Press it into the joints with a grout float or the filling knife, working diagonally across the joints to fill them fully. Wipe away the excess with a damp sponge before it hardens. Once the surface haze has dried (usually 30 to 60 minutes), polish the tile face with a dry cloth.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: The new tile sits proud of the surrounding tiles
attrs.cause: Too much adhesive, or the old adhesive was not fully removed from the wall.
attrs.fix: Remove the tile before the adhesive cures, scrape the wall back further, and re-apply with a thinner adhesive bed.

attrs.cause: The grout was not fully removed from the joint before chiselling, transmitting impact through the joint.
attrs.fix: Remove the affected tiles and replace them. On the next job, rake out the full grout depth on all four sides before breaking the tile.

attrs.symptom: New grout colour does not match
attrs.cause: Standard grout colours vary between manufacturers and batches.
attrs.fix: Take a fragment of existing tile and a photograph of the grout joint colour to the tile merchant to match both. Grout colourant can be used to adjust a close match.
